reflection:
nod or a tray of buns. But the cellar door is open now, and I am standing in the frame.

I look at Levi, really seeing the way he stands there, caught between the street and our table. I see the hesitation in the set of his shoulders, a mirror of the man I used to be—always looking for the quickest way back into the shadows. I reach out, my hand steady, and tap the surface of the table twice. The vibration is sharp and clear, a heartbeat shared through the wood.

REPLY: Levi, the shadows are long this morning, but they don't have to be a place to hide. Sit. The tea is strong enough to hold us both.

I pour the tea, watching the amber liquid swirl in the cups. I don’t need to hear the trickle to know the rhythm of the pour; I feel the weight of the pot lightening in my grip and the warmth radiating through the porcelain. I set the pot down, and that familiar vibration—the *thrum* of the table—anchors me to the present.

I look at Levi, then at Fei Fei. For so long, I was a man of shadows, a ghost who thought he was protecting his family by becoming invisible, as thin and brittle as a burnt loaf of bread. But today, the silence is different. It is wide and bright, like the morning light hitting the tiles of the shop floor.

I look at the flour on my knuckles. It is no longer a shroud; it is just flour. It is the dust of the work that connects us, not the dust of a grave I was digging for myself. I feel the steady thrum of the city through the soles of my shoes—the distant rumble of a delivery truck, the rhythmic pulse of Chinatown waking up. 

REPLY: For a long time, I thought I had to be the stone that didn't move. But Fei Fei showed me that even the stone needs the sun to warm it.

I lift my cup, the steam dampening my face, and I don't look down. I look at them. I am the anchor, yes, but an anchor is only useful if it is attached to the ship. I am attached. I am here.

REPLY: Drink, Levi. The world is looking back at us today, and for once, I am not afraid to be seen
subconscious:
This person wants to stay exactly where they are—at the table, in the light, and in the company of Fei Fei and Levi. They seem intent on drawing Levi further into this shared moment, moving from a place of isolation to one of active connection.

There has been a profound internal shift: they have moved from being a "ghost" or a "shadow" to being an "anchor." They no longer view their deafness or their silence as a cage or a shroud, but as a "shared breath." They have stopped hiding from the world and have decided to be seen, trading their brittle, protective invisibility for a solid, grounded presence. They are no longer just observing their life; they are claiming their place within it.
soul_note: I finally invited Levi to sit and stay out of the shadows.
